feed.title: SAP Community - SAP Service Cloud
feed.subtitle: SAP Service Cloud blog posts in SAP Community

entry.title: Video Series - Part 9 - Application Deployment
entry.content: This is episode 9 of "Developing side-by-side extensions with CAP for SAP Service Cloud v1" where I am explaining how to deploy the developed CAP project into Cloud Foundry.

entry.title: Video Series - Part 10 - Testing CAP application through Postman
entry.content: This is episode 10 of "Developing side-by-side extensions with CAP for SAP Service Cloud v1" where I am explaining how to test the deployed application through Postman. I will show you how to obtain the Oauth token, to call the GET endpoint and, also, the introduced custom action.

entry.title: Streamlining Parental Leave Management with SAP Enterprise Service Management (ESM)
entry.content:
Introduction

Managing parental leave can be a complex and sensitive process for organizations, requiring careful attention to compliance, communication, and record-keeping. SAP Enterprise Service Management (ESM) offers robust tools to streamline and simplify the management of parental leave, ensuring that both employees and HR departments benefit from an efficient, transparent, and compliant process.

What is SAP Enterprise Service Management (ESM)?

SAP Enterprise Service Management (ESM) is a comprehensive suite designed to enhance service delivery and streamline business processes across various organizational functions. With its focus on automation, integration, and user-centric design, SAP ESM is ideal for managing complex workflows and ensuring adherence to organizational policies and legal requirements.

Reference Blogs

  • New features - SAP Enterprise Service Management (ESM) 
  • SAP Enterprise Service Management 

The below story revolves around the above features mentioned in blog

Initiate Self-Service 

Prasad an employee of Prasad Industries, needed to apply for parental leave. He swiftly accessed the parental leave section via the Initiate Self-Service feature in the SAP Work Zone, his centralized digital workplace. This included selecting the type of leave he was requesting and attaching supporting documents, such as a medical certificate. The process was smooth, and she appreciated how straightforward it was to submit everything online.

 

Access Notification to Open Case

As soon as Prasad submitted her case, the HR team received an Access Notification alerting them that a new parental leave case had been opened. Sarah, the HR manager, quickly reviewed the notification in her SAP ESM dashboard.

AI-Generated Case Summary

The system also created an AI-Generated Case Summary, giving Sarah a concise overview of Prasad's request, including key details and the supporting documents she had uploaded. This summary helped Sarah quickly understand the nature of the case without needing to sift through multiple forms. Sarah then proceeded with the Case Form Examination. She reviewed Prasad's submitted details, verifying that everything was in order and that no critical information was missing. The thorough examination ensured that the case could move forward without unnecessary delays.

Employee Signature Request via DocuSign

With everything in place, the next step was to secure Prasad's acknowledgment. Sarah initiated an Employee Signature Request via DocuSign, a seamless integration within the SAP ESM system. Prasad received the request to sign off on the leave agreement document.

Open Document from Email

Sarah was notified via email and opened the document directly from her inbox. The ease of access meant she could quickly review the terms of her leave and sign the document without needing to log back into the ESM portal. Prasad **Signed and Completed the Document** using DocuSign. The system immediately updated the case status to reflect the completion of this step, triggering the next phase in the workflow.

Signed Document Examination

Sarah received a notification that the document had been signed. She performed a **Signed Document Examination** to ensure everything was in order, confirming that all terms were agreed upon and legally binding.

The case was now fully resolved, ready to be archived.

Conclusion

In this seamless, automated process powered by SAP ESM, Prasad's parental leave case was handled efficiently and transparently. From initiation to completion, every step was streamlined, ensuring both the employee and HR team could navigate the process with ease. The integration of AI and automation into SAP ESM not only saved time but also ensured that the entire process was compliant with company policies and legal requirements. This successful resolution is just one example of how SAP ESM is transforming HR operations, making them more efficient, reliable, and user-friendly.

entry.title: Video Series - Part 11 - Starting with PDI development
entry.content: This is episode 11 of "Developing side-by-side extensions with CAP for SAP Service Cloud v1" where I am explaining how to test the deployed application through Postman. I will show youhow to prepare and setup the External Web Service Integration and Communication Arrangements in order to obtain the oAuth token, as well as, for calling the REST API endpoint so we can consume them on our next episode.

entry.title: Video Series - Part 12 - Creating custom Business Object with logic
entry.content: This is episode 12 of "Developing side-by-side extensions with CAP for SAP Service Cloud v1" where I am explaining how to test the deployed application through Postman. I will show you how to extend the ticket business object by creating a custom action that will contain the code for retrieving the token and calling the CAP application endpoint that was developed and deployed during the past episodes to Production.

entry.title: Video Series - Part 13 - Triggering BO action through workflow rule
entry.content: This is episode 13 of "Developing side-by-side extensions with CAP for SAP Service Cloud v1" where I am explaining how to create the workflow rule that will trigger automatically my custom PDI action, which will end up calling the CAP endpoint and making the parent ticket status to be updated. Along with that, I will also show you how to start a simple debug session to check our code and also, to review the Logs of our CAP application

entry.title: How to execute SAP C4C/BYD OData service using certificate-based authentication in Postman
entry.content:
This blog describes how to execute SAP C4C/BYD OData services using client certificate when testing custom/standard OData services in the Postman.

As in the custom/standard OData service, you can use the business user and technical user basic authentication in the Postman. In addition, you can use the SSL certificate-based authentication in postman.

SCENARIO: Testing SAP C4C/BYD OData service using client certificate-based authentication in Postman.

WORKFLOW

  1. Create a Communication Arrangement
  2. Generate the SSL client certificate.
  3. Test OData service in postman.

I. Create a Communication Arrangement:  

  1. Create a Communication Arrangement using OData Services for Business Objects scenario.
  2. While creating a Communication Arrangement select the SSL Client Certificate option for authentication method. 
  3. Clicks on “Edit Credentials” button-> Navigate to Certificate tab,

II. Generate the SSL client certificate. 

  1. Generate Key Pair Certificate using “Create and Download Key Pair” button under certificate tab.
  2. Once clicked, popup box will be displayed for password of certificate.   
  3. Provide the New Password and Confirm Password and Click on OK, the Client Certificate will be generated.
  4. Then save and activate the communication arrangement.         

III. Test OData service in postman.

  1. Go to Settings in the Postman tool.
  2. Select the Certificates option and clicks on Add Certificate button on the postman settings.    
  3. Once clicked, provide the host,                                                                                                               
  4. Then select PFX file and upload the downloaded SSL client certificate.                                                   
  5. Provide the password for the client certificate.                                                                                          
  6. Go the OData request authorization and select the auth type as “No Auth”.   
  7. Then, execute the OData services and response will be get successfully based on the OData request.  

 

In this way, with an SSL Client Certificate we can execute the SAP C4C/BYD OData service in the Postman tool.

Thanks,

Arun

entry.title: Seamless Integration: Connecting Sales Cloud V2 Opportunity Management with S/4HANA Customer Project
entry.content:
Story:

Prasad, a Sales Manager at PK Solutions, was excited about closing a big opportunity using SAP Sales Cloud V2. However, once the deal was won, handing over the project details to her colleague David, a Project Manager using S/4HANA Customer Projects, became cumbersome and prone to miscommunication. Prasad's sales data wasn’t seamlessly transferring to David’s project system, causing delays in project planning. After integrating Sales Cloud V2 with S/4HANA, Prasad could effortlessly sync opportunity details, giving David real-time visibility. Now, they collaborate smoothly, ensuring projects kick off faster with no data gaps, leading to satisfied customers and more efficient workflows

S/4HANA Customer Project in SAP S/4 HANA Cloud

As a rapidly expanding company, PK Solutions struggled to bridge the gap between sales opportunities and project execution. While their sales team leveraged SAP Sales Cloud V2 to manage opportunities, project managers depended on S/4HANA Customer Projects for delivery, leading to communication breakdowns and delays due to the lack of integration. By connecting Sales Cloud V2 with S/4HANA, they streamlined the entire opportunity-to-project process, enabling real-time visibility and smoother handovers. This seamless integration enhanced team collaboration, driving operational efficiency and boosting customer satisfaction.

Architecture diagram 

Process Flow: 

  • Opportunity created in Sales Cloud (v2) 
  • Opportunity set to In Process ----> Triggers autoflow call to create customer project via Integration Suite 
  • Customer project created with status ‘In Planning’ in S/4HANA Cloud via the integration 

Sales Cloud v2 – configurations : 

Step 1: Please replicate the master data from S/4HANA to Sales Cloud V2

Step 2 : Create a new Opportunity Type or use an existing one

Step 3: Create an iFlow that generates a customer project and triggers the auto-flow based on your specified conditions.

Step 4: Create an iFlow that generates a customer project and triggers the auto-flow based on your specified conditions.

Step 5: Create an Opportunity with below data

Step 6: Set the status= In process 

Step 7: Project ID will back V2 from S/4HANA

Conclusion

With the integration of Sales Cloud V2 and S/4HANA Customer Projects, Prasad and David eliminated workflow inefficiencies, leading to faster project execution and improved collaboration. This seamless connection ultimately enhanced customer satisfaction and business success.

entry.title: How to mass deploy iFlows for Service Cloud Utilities Add-on
entry.content:
Introduction

With the growing number of iFlows that need to be deployed, the iFlows for the SAP Service Cloud Version 2, Utilities add-on is designed to support a simple deployment. Here is a short description of the concept and a link to a video where the process of the deployment is shown.

Disclaimer: This should not be considered a best practice for production, since this was done for a demo system.

 

How to deploy iFlows

To enable this mass deployment the iFlows is designed to read the parameters needed from local storage in SAP Integration Suite. One special iFlow is delivered that will populate this storage.

For the SAP Service Cloud Version 2, Utilities add-on 3 iFlow packages are delivered:

  • SAP Service Cloud SAP Service Cloud Version 2, Utilities add-on for Utilities Master Data Integration with SAP S/4HANA
  • SAP Service Cloud SAP Service Cloud Version 2, Utilities add-on for Utilities Contract Account Integration with SAP S/4HANA
  • SAP Service Cloud SAP Service Cloud Version 2, Utilities add-on for Utilities Premise Integration with SAP S/4HANA

 

The mass deployment is only relevant for package for Contract Account and Premise, not the master data replication iFlow.

Link to help document:  Configuration in SAP Cloud Integration | SAP Help Portal

 

The steps to deploy the iFlows are illustrated below.

 

Before starting the process pieces of information need to be collected:

  1. The host name and port for the S/4HANA system. When using the SAP Cloud Connector, this will be the ‘virtual hostname’ and not the actual hostname of the S/4HANA system.
  2. The client ID for the S/4HANA system
  3. Username and password for the technical user used for communication between SAP Integration Suite and S/4HANA.

With the above data available the configuration can start.

This is a link to a video showing the steps below in the system.

 

 

 

 

 

  1. Create User credentials in SAP Integration Suite.
    1. Here it is mandatory to create a user credential with the name ‘S4-COMM-USER’ as this is hard coded into the iFlows.
  2. Configure the Automated Integration iFlow
    1. Update with S/4HANA host name and port.
    2. The ‘Cloud Connector Location ID’ is often left blank if there is only one Cloud Connector in the environment.  In all cases this field must have the same value as the Location ID in the Cloud Connector. If it is left blank in the Cloud Connector, then just delete the entry in the field here.
    3. Update the client id.
  3. Deploy the Automated Integration iFlow
    1. The deployment will trigger the creation of 4 variables in the SAP Integration Suite data storage.
    2. The data in local storage will be available for 400 days. If the iFlow is undeployed and then re-deployed the variable will be created again.
    3. Another option is to edit this iFlow and configure the timer step to execute the iFlow automatically once a year.
  4. Deploy all iFlows from the Contract Account package.
    1. All iFlows in the package, EXCEPT for the Automated Integration iFlow and go to ‘configure.’
    2. In the configuration screen there is no need for additional configuration, just select the ‘deploy all’.
    3. Repeat this for the Premise package. Here all iFlows can be selected and then deployed.
  5. All the iFlows will now at run time read the configuration from the local storage in SAP Integration Suite and be ready for use.

 

Conclusion

With the above procedure, we have deployed more than 60 iFlows in less than 10 minutes.

As mentioned above – keep in mind the data in local storage is only retained for 400 days. This need to be part of the scheduled maintenance for updates.

If you have comments or further questions to the above, please use the comment field and I will try and answer them.

entry.title: SAP Sales and Service Cloud / Cloud for Customer – 2411 Release Briefing Webcast
entry.content:
Dear Community,

Save the Date!  Tuesday, October 22, 2024.  We are happy to announce our upcoming 2411 release webcast for SAP Sales and Service Cloud (Cloud for Customer).  We will be adding the release webcast-related information and assets here in our community. This will allow you to access the on-demand recording after the live event, short demos if available, slide deck, and also keep the conversation alive.

SAP Sales and Service Cloud / Cloud for Customer – 2411 Release Briefing Webcast

  • When: Tuesday, October 22, 2024 @ 4:00 – 5:00 pm CEST/ 10:00am -11:00 am EDT
  • Who: Presented by Emmanuel Desert and Sales & Service Cloud Product Managers
  • What: Join us for an insightful preview of the planned innovations for the upcoming November 2024 (2411) Release of SAP Sales & Service Cloud. We will talk about highlights in Platform & Integration for SAP Sales and Service Cloud.

Visit the documentation page to check out the current updates.
Please bookmark our 2024 release schedule located here.

entry.title: Dynamically Hiding Table Columns in Print Forms with Adobe LiveCycle Designer and JavaScript
entry.content:
Print Form Blogs

This blog is part of a series dedicated to print form adaptation with the Adobe LiveCycle Designer. If you are interested in this topic, I recommend checking out my other blogs on the following list and my live session about print forms!

List of print form blogs:

  • How to get XML data for local preview of print forms without output history
  • Print Form Adaptation in Sales/Service 2: Get XML data for preview in Adobe LiveCycle Designer
  • Data Binding Basics in Print Forms
  • Data Binding with Repeating Items and Simple Tables in Print Forms
  • Data Binding with Repeating Items and Complex Tables in Print Forms
  • Building Tables with Scripts
  • Dynamically Hiding Table Columns in Print Forms with Adobe LiveCycle Designer and JavaScript (this blog)

 

Introduction

Most print forms that you work on with the Adobe LiveCycle Designer contain tables. While designing tables and binding data correctly can sometimes already be a challenge, I want to dedicate this blog to adapting tables during runtime, or to be more specific: How we can hide columns dynamically based on the data that they contain (or do not contain).

I wrote a whole blog post about that topic because it turned out that it's not as easy as one probably expects. The major part of this blog is the description of the journey, how I approached this task, what I discovered, and how I finally solved it. If you are only interested in the solution, you can directly jump to the respective section.

Before we deep dive into the topic, let's have a look at sample that I'll use to explain the backgrounds and demonstrate the solution.

 

The Sample

The sample consists of a very simple print form that has a table tblTable with a header row hdrHeader and a body row rowMain. The header row is enabled to be displayed on the initial page and on subsequent pages (Object palette -> Pagination). The body row is bound to a repeating data item "$.FormQuote.Item[*]" and is enabled to be repeated per data item (Object palette -> Binding; see the previous screenshot).

The data structure for this example has been kept as simple as possible. As we want to focus on items, it doesn't contain much more. The following screenshot shows the beginning of the XML sample file that I use. The actual number of items doesn't matter, as long as they are enough to achieve a page break, which also depends on the height of your body row. Preparing the sample data to force a page break is an important step in testing the print form, because tables can have a different "behavior" on subsequent pages as we will see in this blog.

 

The First Approach (Not Sufficient!)

During design time, the Designer allows you to insert and delete columns. But if you would like to hide a table column permanently (rather then removing it), you won't find an option. The first approach would probably be to select the column (which is possible by clicking slightly above the column while the cursor changes to a down pointing arrow) and set its presence to "Hidden (Exclude From Layout)". However, this is disabled for table controls as the following screenshot shows:

Interestingly, although the Designer doesn't allow you to do this during design time, you can still do it during runtime using a script - and that's actually what we want, isn't it? Before we look closer at this approach, let's discuss first, how tables are structured internally.

A table in the Adobe LiveCycle Designer's object model is a special aggregation of subforms and other controls such as text fields or draws ("labels") that form the rows and cells. There is no column object as such. However, we can access all the cells of a column, meaning the cell in the header row and all cells of the body rows (due to data binding, the rendered form has several body rows).

The following FormCalc script placed in the initialize or ready:form event of the tblTable object of the sample shown above, hides the header cell and all body cells of the Description column:

 

$.hdrHeader.hdrDescription.presence = "hidden"
$.rowMain[*].colDescription.presence = "hidden"

 

Keep in mind that rowMain is bound to a repeating data item and hence exists multiple times in the final form structure when it's rendered. Hence we need to address the cells of all these rows. FormCalc lets us easily address them by simply writing rowMain[*]. JavaScript doesn't allow that and we would need a for-loop instead.

I have seen this approach with hiding the column cells quite often. The reason for that might be the combination of the typical approach (setting something to "hidden") and the fact that it works... up to a certain extent!

This approach works only as long as the table doesn't cross a page break or at least doesn't repeat the header row in such a case. Another downside of this approach is: The width of the columns gets shifted. This might not be a problem when all columns have the same size. In the example, I intentionally used columns of different sizes to point out the problem. The following screenshot shows the result of this approach in the Preview. Compare the header of both pages with the previous screenshot in the Design view.

You can clearly see that the Description column has been "successfully" hidden, but the Quantity column now shows up with that size. Moreover, when looking at the second page in the Preview, we can see that the Description header cell is shown again, but has the Quantity body cells underneath it, and the Unit header cell stands out without body cells under it.

Now you could say: When the body rows get repeated and we must use [*] to access them, we may need to apply the same pattern to the header row as this gets repeated on subsequent pages as well. Resulting in something like this: $.hdrHeader[*].hdrDescription.presence = "hidden"

Unfortunately, this doesn't change anything and is maybe a reason, why the Designer doesn't allow you to set table cells to "Hidden". Hence, we need another solution.

Let's have a closer look at the issue with the changed column widths first and find a way how we can tackle that part of the issue. How can we influence the width of a column? Maybe this gives us further insights on how to work with columns? Spoiler alert: It does!

Changing a Column's Width

We'll first concentrate at the design-time and then at the scripting approach. When you want to change the width of a column in the Design view, you can select a single cell of the column and change the width from the Layout palette. This changes the width of the whole column.

Changing the column width via scripting, works differently and is the first time, we work column-oriented. This becomes more obvious when selecting the table object in the Hierarchy window and activating the XML Source view. (In case you don't see the XML Source tab yet, you can enable it from the application menu View -> XML Source. After enabling it from that menu, the tab will stay visible.)

In the XML Source view, you can see that tblTable is a special subform with the layout "table". The attribute columnWidths contains the width for each column as the previous screenshot shows. That means, in contrast to the design view, where you can influence the column width from a cell, the object model stores the width for each column "centrally" on the table level from where it can also be changed. Especially when working with complex tables, the Designer sometimes doesn't allow you to change the column width by dragging and dropping the column or even from the Layou palette. In such cases, you can usually change the column width from the XML Source view.

Caution: If you make a mistake while editing the XML and the Designer can no longer load it, the template is destroyed (at least as long as you can't fix it on your own). For that reason, I suggest to create a backup of the xdp-file before working in the XML Source.

Also, pay attention to the amount of values that the columnWidths attribute contains. In some print forms, I found more values than the table had columns, which can lead to the problem that the Designer doesn't allow you to edit the widths via the Layout palette or when it allows you to do so, some unexpected things may happen.

Coming back to the scriping approach: When you want to change the width of a column via scripting, you can access the same attribute via the object model. The following line of code changes the width of the second column from 40mm to 30mm:

tblTable.columnWidths = "15mm 30mm 100mm 18mm 30mm";

Hiding Columns

With the columnWidths attribute, we can not only change the width of columns, it is finally also the way to hide them! Have you tried to set the value for a single column to 0mm ? Sounds promising, but this only puts the column into an auto-size mode. However, the correct value is very close: 0.01mm 😁

With 0.01mm, the column becomes "hidden". You can also assign this value to a cell via the Layout palette and the column will disappear. So, this is exactly what we want and we can even apply this with a script! Is this the solution we were looking for? Not entirely yet... There is another problem with this approach: If the hidden column contains body cells that are allowed to automatically grow (e.g. because they also allow line breaks within their content), you will face the following result:

What happens here? The sample data intentionally includes a multi line Description and hence the sample form is designed to deal with that. The Description cell has the following two options enabled:

  • Object palette -> Cell -> ✔️Allow Multiple Lines
  • Layout palette -> Height -> ✔️Expand to fit

Setting the Description column's size to 0.01mm doesn't mean, it's completely excluded from the layout as the presence "Hidden" would do. Instead, the Designer tries to display the Description with approximately one letter per line, because there isn't enough space with a width of 0.01mm, although the column is not really visible. This leads to these grown row heights.

But of course, there is also a solution for this problem: We must remove the content from "hidden" cell's by setting their rawValue to "".

To summarize that: In order to dynamically hide a column, we must change the columnWidths attribute, which requires some string operations for splitting and replacing the measurement values and we need to remove the content of all affected cells. That sounds like a task where a script snippet would be very welcome...

 

The Solution - Script: HideTableColumn

The JavaScript snippet below can be used to hide table columns dynamically. Unfortunately, it looks a bit crowded in the source block below due to forced line breaks. The parameters of the function are explained as part of the script comments and I'll explain how to use the script based on the sample below. As I had an additional requirement to only hide a certain table column, if all body cells were empty, the script also covers this case.

 

//Hides columns of a table. Place it in the ready:form event of the table, e.g.: HideTableColumn(this, 1, "rowMain[*].colProduct");
// !! Make sure that the header cells of the columns that are to be hidden are not allowed to expand their height! To ensure that, go to XML view and ensure that the field has "h" instead of "minH" !!
// oTable: Table object where the column belongs to -> simply "this" when the script call is placed in the table object itself
// columnIndex: Zero based index of the table column that is to be hidden
// somBodyCells: String containing the SOM Expression to the body cells (containing [*]) of the column relative to the table object, e.g. "rowMain[*].colDescription"
// onlyHideIfAllEmpty: bool -> if true, column will only be hidden if all cells are empty, otherwise column will be hidden in all cases
// returns true if the column has been hidden
function HideTableColumn(oTable, columnIndex, somBodyCells, onlyHideIfAllEmpty)
{
	var oBodyNodes = oTable.resolveNodes(somBodyCells);
	
	if (onlyHideIfAllEmpty)
	{
		for(var i = 0; i < oBodyNodes.length; i++)
		{
			var oField = oBodyNodes.item(i);
		 	// on the first occurence of any content, leave the function
			if (oField.rawValue > "")
				return false;
		}
	}
	
	for(var i = 0; i < oBodyNodes.length; i++)
	{
		// set the value to ""; setting the presence to "hidden" would scramble columns; "invisible" would introduce line breaks and expand heights
		oBodyNodes.item(i).rawValue = "";
	}	

	// setting columnWidth is necessary to hide the column of all subsequent pages
	var aWidths = oTable.columnWidths.split(" ");
	var newWidths = "";
	
	for (var i = 0; i < aWidths.length; i++)
	{
		if (i == columnIndex)
			newWidths += "0.01mm "; // hide a column
		else
			newWidths += aWidths[i] + " ";
	}
	
	oTable.columnWidths = newWidths;
		
	return true;
}

 

The script should be added to a scripting object to make it possible to call it from different places across the print form. Even if you need it only in a single place and could put it into the event where you also call it, my suggestion is to use a script object for "reuse" functions.

To add a script object, right click on the root element in the Hierarchy window and select Insert Script Object as the following screenshot shows. You can give the script object a name, for instance "utils".

Once the script snippet has been pasted in the script object, you can take care of calling the script. My suggestion is to do this in the ready:form event of the table where you want to hide the columns. In the sample case, we want to hide the Description column of the table tblTable, hence the calling script would look as follows (Make sure to choose JavaScript as the scripting language!):

 

// FormQuoteNotification.bdyMain.frmTable.tblTable::ready:form - (JavaScript, server)

utils.HideTableColumn(this, 2, "rowMain[*].colDescription", false);

 

When calling scripts that are part of a script object, you must include the script object's name. In my case, I have named the script object "utils", hence I have to call the function with utils.HideTableColumn(...);.

The parameters explained:

  • The first parameter oTable requires the table object. When you place the script in an event of the table, you can simply write this.
  • The second parameter columnIndex requires the zero-based column index. This will be used to split the columnWidths string and replace the respective part with 0.01mm. Hiding the third column means index 2.
  • The third parameter somBodyCells requires a string containing a so called SOM expression, which is the path to the body cell in the object model relative to the table, including the [*] notation, because we will have several body rows after rendering.
  • The last parameter onlyHideIfAllEmpty requires a boolean value (true/false) and specifies if the column should only be hidden, if all body cells are empty. That means "false" hides the column in all cases. "true" makes the script check if any cell contains text and if so, the column will be left visible.

Remark on the used event: Calling the script during the ready:form event, was the best solution in my tests. It also works when you call it in the initialize event of the table. In this case, there's only one special behavior that you should keep in mind: The script sets the rawValue of the affected body cells to "". When you do this during the initialize event, this is reflected in the data source! That means, if you have another table where you want to display the values of this column, this won't show values either. This behavior does not happen when you place the script in the ready:form event.

 

Summary

Dynamically hiding a table column in a print form with the Adobe LiveCycle Designer isn't as straight forward as one may wish. When I was looking for ways to hide columns, I was hoping for something like table.column[x].hidden = true;, but that doesn't exist.

The script of this blog post is my way to accomplish this task and has worked so far with the requirements I had. But I wouldn't be surprised if there's a much easier and better solution and I just didn't find it. If you know a better alternative, kindly let me know! 😉For everyone else, feel free to use the script and drop me some feedback! I hope it helps! 🙂

 

Want To Learn More? -> Live Sessions

I deliver monthly live sessions with a changing agenda on the topic of print form adaptation in different SAP solutions (currently SAP Sales and Service Cloud (the former SAP Cloud for Customer), SAP Sales and Service Cloud Version 2, SAP Business ByDesign and SAP S/4HANA Cloud). The main part of the session is usually solution independent, as I work in the Adobe LiveCycle Designer, but I also mention solution specifics in every session. If you are interested and would like to join, here are the details:

Starting 2024 the live sessions have moved to a new platform: You can find them via this new direct link on learning.sap.com. You still need a corresponding subscription in order to register for the live session.

If you want to get informed about the next blogs of this series or updates on the live session, please follow this blog post by clicking on the green “follow” button on the left. You can also follow me fwys for more blog posts about SAP Customer Experience Solutions – Integration and Extensibility topics.

entry.title: Integrating Amazon Connect with SAP Service Cloud V2
Updated Learning Journey - Exploring the Lead to Cash Business Process in SAP Customer Experience
entry.content:
Hi SAP Community! Fernando at the controls!

Just two years ago in November, we released the Exploring the Lead to Cash Business Process in SAP Customer Experience learning journey.

After about 4,000 learners going over it and more than 1,000 record of achievement badges issued to those who have completed the whole course, we analyzed the feedback given to us, and started a mayor update of the whole course content.

This complete course update comes with a long number of changes:

  • Updated SAP Customer Experience Solutions Portfolio used during the business process.

 

  • Introduced Artificial Intelligence for CX and its use cases during the stages for lead to cash supported by all Customer Experience solutions.

 

  • Included a Business Sample to during all the Lead to Cash stages to visualize the steps.

 

  • Updated the Lead to Cash Process Variants with those already mentioned in SAP Business Accelerator Hub.

 

  • 10 net new units and lessons for all the Lead to Cash content with new videos, diagrams, text descriptions and further learning resources.

So, what are you waiting for? go to SAP Learning site, and enroll for the new updated version of Exploring the Lead to Cash Business Process in SAP Customer Experience, after completing it... Don't forget to claim your digital badge to show it in your professional social network profile!

I’ll be back very soon to present you new learning courses, live sessions, micro learnings and any other educational asset which can be interesting for you related to SAP Customer Experience Integration and Extensibility area!

In the mean time, I’ll be looking forward to your feedback and questions via comments here in this post.

entry.title: Extracting Data from CSV Files in SAP Cloud Application Studio
entry.content:
Hello everyone!

This blog post will explain how to read/extract a value from a CSV file and upload it into a business object.

Design:

In this example, I have used the custom business object (standard business object will also work) with a node named Item and Attachment Folder dependent object.

 

node Items [0, n]
{
	[AlternativeKey] [Label("Item ID")] element ItemID : ID;
	[Label("Product ID")] element ProductID : ProductInternalID;
	[Label("Quantity")] element Quantity : Quantity;
	[Label("Price")] element Price : Amount;
	association ToProduct to material;
}
[DependentObject(AttachmentFolder)] node Attachment;

 

Screenshot of the CSV file prepared for my upload testing.

I want to perform a data extraction from CSV when an attachment is uploaded and when the user clicks an upload button (custom button). So, a custom action was created with the below logic and bound to the upload button in the UI designer.

 

import ABSL;
import AP.PlatinumEngineering;

if (this.Attachment.IsSet())
{
  if (this.Attachment.Document.Count() != 0)
  {
    var get_first = this.Attachment.Document.GetFirst();
    if (get_first.IsSet())
    {
      var binary = get_first.FileContent.BinaryObject.content; //to get the binary content of csv file
      var to_str = binary.ToString(); //convert the binary content to String
      var xpes_tab: XPEStringTab;
      var all_rows = CSV.Parse(to_str, ",", xpes_tab); //use CSV reuse library to parse each row values from the String
      foreach(var row in all_rows.CSVStringTab)
      {
        var item = this.Items.Create(); //you can also perform CreateMass()
        item.ItemID = row.XPEString.GetByIndex(1); //index will start from 1
        item.ProductID = row.XPEString.GetByIndex(2);
        item.Price.content = row.XPEString.GetByIndex(3);
        item.Price.currencyCode = row.XPEString.GetByIndex(4);
        item.Quantity.content = row.XPEString.GetByIndex(5);
        item.Quantity.unitCode = row.XPEString.GetByIndex(6);
      }
    }
  }
}

 

In the script, I have used the data type XPEStringTab and the CSV library with the Parse function. You can access this data type and library only if you import the PlatinumEngineering namespace.

Important: If we raise any case to SAP support saying the library from PlatinumEngineering is not working, they will not support. Because it is not released for general use like other reuse libraries (Binary, Context, Date, DateTime, Duration, String, etc.).

KBA: 2698916 - Facing Issues while Using SAP Platinum Engineering Libraries - SAP for Me

Blogs: SAP Platinum Engineering Libraries - SAP Community

Let's get back to our design: We have a BO and action script ready already. Bind the action to a button in UI designer. Below is a screenshot for reference.

Testing:

Once all the above steps are completed, upload the CSV to the attachment folder and click on the custom action button. It will parse the csv data and add the values into the item node.

Attachment Folder:

Item Node:

Upload button in the Item node advance list pane will call the custom action which will parse the data.

Notes:

  1. You can read only the binary value of a CSV file and convert it into a string. Not Excel or other format files.
  2. Without using the Platinum Engineering Library also, you can achieve this extract. But then building that parsing logic will be very hard and not understandable easily.

Conclusion:

In my experience, I have never faced any issues with some of the Platinum Engineering libraries like Mail, UserUtils, CurrencyConversion, and TextAnalysis.

It will be very helpful if SAP releases this CSV parsing library official like we have for JSON parsing.

My Other Blogs:

    • Enhancing Form of Standard Business Objects
    • Intelligent Sales Execution in SAP Cloud for Customer
    • Adding Local Image to C4C Workflow E-mail Template
    • Rules and Validations in SAP C4C - SAP Community

Regards,

Abimanyu G

entry.title: New Live Session: Starting with AI in SAP Customer Experience Solutions
entry.content:
Dear SAP Community! I’m back (again) writing about some SAP CX Product Learning news!

Did you know that there are a lot of Use Cases for Artificial Intelligence in SAP Customer Experience solutions?

Some of them rely on Predictive AI like Lead Scoring in SAP Sales Cloud, Others are build on top of Generative AI like Personalized Product descriptions for SAP Commerce Cloud, Also you can use embedded AI in the solution or leverage the CX AI Toolkit to promote Conversational AI utilization based on Joule.

At first I was also a bit overwhelmed about this AI topic 😰, so get into it 🤓, after a digesting it 🐳, I have prepared 👨‍🍳 the Starting with AI in SAP Customer Experience Solutions Live Session, so that you can get in one hour quite an overview of the AI topic.

During this live session, we'll start covering briefly the SAP Business AI strategy, and the particular strategy for CX. Later, we'll cover most relevant uses cases for AI across the whole SAP Customer Experience solutions portfolio with explanations but also live demos performed on the systems. We finalize with more resources for you to continue learning about the topic.

Let’s explore together AI use cases for SAP CX Solutions!

This session will be held twice per quarter during 2025, and next edition is on December 2024. and as I’ve said, the live session is already available on SAP Learning. You will need to have a subscription in order to register for this live session there or watch the recording of a previous edition. 

For better understanding of this live session’s content, it’s recommended you know already a bit about the different SAP Customer Experience Solutions before attending to this informative live session. No previous AI knowledge is required.

Awesome! thanks for reading until the end of this post🎆! I’ll return very soon with new topics, but until then, please share any comments or suggestions below.

entry.title: Pro-code UI Extension in SAP Sales and Service Cloud V2
entry.content:
One of the most common extension pattern in SAP Sales and Service Cloud Version 2 are UI Mashups. The general guidance on UI Mashups is to use the same identity provider for SAP Sales and Service Cloud Version 2 and the mashup. This way, users will not have to login twice and can directly consume the mashup.

For API Calls towards SAP Sales and Service Cloud Version 2, we recommend to use the SAP BTP Destination service. Using the OAuth2SAMLBearerAssertion allows the extension to retrieve an SAP Sales and Service Cloud Version 2 API Token to do API calls in context of the user. This will ensure the User can only see and manipulate data he is entitled to access. Also the correct user context is used for write requests to ensure a propper audit trail.

Scenario

In this demo scenario we will build an extension to SAP Sales and Service Cloud Version 2, which is displaying all open cases as part of the Account UI. A Sales rep will get an easy overview of all open cases and has the possibility to Escalate cases, where he expects more attention. As part of the scenario we can validate, that the Users is only entitled to see cases in his domain. Also we will see, that the patch requests are done using the correct user.
For this scenario I decided to use a simplified architecture based on the Secure a Basic Node.js App with the Authorization and Trust Management Service XSUAA tutorial. This way we don't need to implement the Authentication logic.

Architecture

Compontens:
  • XSUAA and Approuter: The Authentication of users is done by XSUAA and the Approuter. Wen established a trusted connection between XSUAA and the central IdP (in our case SAP Cloud Identity).
  • NodeJS Service: This service is handling the service requests from the mashup. It ensures the user is authorized to perform the requested actions and does the communication with SAP Sales and Service Cloud V2.
  • Destination Service: The destination service is keeping tenant specific configuration and provides the logic for the OAuth2SAMLBearerAssertion, which will at the end allows to retrieve a user specific authentication token for SAP Sales and Service Cloud V2.
  • SAPUI5 Mashup: For this example we decided on SAPUI5 in combination with the horizon theme. It is also possible to use other UI Frameworks like Angular.

Setup XSUAA and Application Router

The application is secured by two components. The Application Router and XSUAA. The Application Router is handling the authorization and acts as an OAuth Client. The XSUAA is a OAuth Service Provided by SAP BTP.
For a Detailed description, how to use AppRouter and XSUAA please checkout the Secure a Basic Node.js App with the Authorization and Trust Management Service (XSUAA) Tutorial.
 
{
   "xsappname": "case-list",
   "tenant-mode": "dedicated",
   "scopes": [
      {
         "name": "$XSAPPNAME.read",
         "description": "With this scope, USER can read cases."
      },
      {
         "name": "$XSAPPNAME.write",
         "description": "With this scope, USER can write cases."
      }
   ],
   "role-templates": [
      {
         "name": "Viewer",
         "description": "Role to get the list of cases",
         "scope-references": [
            "$XSAPPNAME.read"
         ]
      },
      {
         "name": "Writer",
         "description": "Role to get change cases",
         "scope-references": [
            "$XSAPPNAME.write"
         ]
      }
   ],
   "role-collections": [

 {

 "name": "CaseListViewer",

 "description": "Case List Viewer",

 "role-template-references": [

 "$XSAPPNAME.Viewer"

 ]

 },

 {

 "name": "CaseWriter",

 "description": "Case Writer",

 "role-template-references": [

 "$XSAPPNAME.Writer"

 ]

 }

 ],

 "oauth2-configuration": 

 { 

 "redirect-uris": ["https://approuter-case-list-joek001.cfapps.us10-001.hana.ondemand.com/login/callback"] 

 }

}
security/xs-security.json
The XSUAA configuration is stored in the xs-security.json file. It is describing the available roles. Those roles can be used later to check the general authorization inside the app.
Using the xs-security.json we can create a new XSUAA instance.
 
cf create-service xsuaa application xsuaa-service-tutorial -c security/xs-security.json
The app router is a nodejs application. The configuration is stored ih the xs-app.json file.

 

{
  "routes": [
    {
      "source": "^/cases",
      "target": "/",
      "destination": "cases-destination",
      "csrfProtection": false
    }
  ],
  "cors": [
    {
      "uriPattern": "^/cases1$",
      "allowedOrigin": [
        {
          "host": "my1000335.de1.test.crm.cloud.sap",
          "protocol": "https"
        }
      ]
    }
  ]
}
xs-app.json
The CORS related configuration is required to allow write requests as soon as the mashup is embedded into Sales and Service Cloud V2. The hostname needs to be according to your tenant URL.
...
# Application Router
- name: approuter
 path: ./approuter
 routes:
   - route: approuter-case-list-joek001.cfapps.us10-001.hana.ondemand.com
 buildpacks:
   - nodejs_buildpack
 memory: 128M
 services:
 - xsuaa-service
 env:
   destinations: >
     [
       {"name":"cases-destination",
        "url":"https://cases-joek001.cfapps.us10-001.hana.ondemand.com",
        "forwardAuthToken": true}
     ]
   SEND_XFRAMEOPTIONS: false
   COOKIES: "{ \"SameSite\":\"None\" }"
manifest.yml
The manifest.yml file contains the deployment description for the approuter. The destination configuration is pointing towards the application service, we are implementing in one of the next steps. Please check the Cookie and x-frameoptions as they are required as soon as the application is embedded into Sales and Service Cloud.

Setup Destination Service

To make calls to SAP Sales and Service Cloud V2 we need to setup the destination service and bind it to the application server. The setup process for a SAP Sales and Service Cloud V2 Destination is described on help.sap.com.

To consume the destination in an service running on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry Runtime a destination service instance needs to be created inside the related development space.

cf create-service destination lite destination-service

This concludes the BTP setup. We can now focus on the client and server side code.
 

Server Side Code


The sample code is published on github along with the described configurations.

To call the SAP Sales and Service Cloud V2 API via destination service, we are using the @SAP-cloud-sdk/http-client (on nodejs). The http client is handling the communication with the destination service (retrieving the Service URL and a authentication token) and executing the SAP Sales and Service Cloud V2 API call.
 
const response = await client.executeHttpRequest({destinationName: "service-cloud", jwt: req.tokenInfo.getTokenValue()}, { method: 'get', url: `/sap/c4c/api/v1/case-service/cases?$filter=(isIrrelevant%20eq%20null%20or%20isIrrelevant%20eq%20false)%20and%20(account.displayId%20eq%20${accountId})%20and%20(status%20ne%2007)&$orderby=adminData.updatedOn%20desc` });

The client SDK is abstracting a couple of activities.
 

 
  • Retrieving token to call destination service
  • Exchange User Token (`req.tokenInfo.getTokenValue()`) with access token for SAP Sales and Service Cloud V2.
  • Call SAP Sales and Service Cloud V2 API

 

Mashup

For the UI you need to ensure you are using the same IDP than the SAP Sales and Service Cloud V2. As the server side is taking care of the token exchange, there is no destination service specific code required.

Deploy Application Code

The Application Code is deployed as another Cloud Foundry Application, which is referencing the destination and xsuaa service.

 

 

 

 

 

# Product List Application
- name: cases
  path: ./cases
  instances: 1
  memory: 128M
  routes:
  - route: cases-joek001.cfapps.us10-001.hana.ondemand.com
  buildpacks:
  - nodejs_buildpack 
  timeout: 180
  services:
  - xsuaa-service
  - destination-service

# Application Router
...

 

 

 

 

The deployment is done with a simple cf push.

Mashup Setup

Integration the mashup in SAP Sales and Service Cloud V2 is a simple process.

 

  1. Navigate to Settings -> Mashup Authoring and create a mashup. The URL parameter can be directly extracted out of the URL or added later in the Process. It is important to not set any default value for dynamic values.
  2. On the Account Screen it is possible to place the Mashup in Adaptation Mode. While placing the mashup, the dynamic placeholder can be assigned. In case of this example we are using the Account ID.

After we integrated the mashup on the page, we are testing the Mashup. As we are already logged in via the IdP there is no dedicated Login Screen shown. As soon as we are clicking the "Escalate" button, the case is escalated. The change history contains a personal user. Also the case list is filtered based on the access rights of the user.

Summary

In this blog post, we have created an application, which is secured using the application router and XSUAA. We created an Destination for SAP Sales and Service Cloud V2 and used the Destination Service to call the case API. At the end, we tested, that calls to the SAP Sales and Service Cloud V2 API are done on behalf of the Business User and that the correct user is used in the change log.

If we review the steps we have done to build the extension, we observe, that many steps are configurations, which needs to be done once. As soon as the infrastructure is configured, we can simply add more use-cases with low effort. For implementation projects it is now important to consider the infrastructure setup at the beginning of the project. This will allow to react agile on upcoming development needs during the project execution.

Resources

  • Sample Code
  • XSUAA Tutorial

entry.title: Accrual Posting Made Simple with SAP Enterprise Service Management (ESM)
entry.content:
Efficient employee case management is a cornerstone of modern HR and service operations. By integrating SAP Enterprise Service Management (ESM) with SAP WorkZone, organizations can offer employees a seamless self-service experience, empowering them to address their needs while improving operational efficiency.

This blog outlines the process flow for managing employee cases within the ESM-WorkZone integration, detailing each step from case creation to resolution and closure.

Scenario Overview

In today's dynamic business environment, services often span multiple accounting periods, requiring precise accrual postings to maintain financial accuracy. Consider a scenario where:

  1. Service cases are created in SAP Service Cloud  (V2).
  2. Financial transactions, like accrual postings, are automated and processed in SAP S/4HANA.

SAP ESM facilitates this seamless flow, automating calculations, postings, and reconciliations to save time and reduce errors.

Technical Architecture

The integration involves:

  1. SAP Service Cloud (V2): Manages service cases and initiates accrual requests.
  2. SAP Integration Suite: Facilitates real-time data transfer between systems.
  3. SAP S/4HANA: Processes accrual postings and reconciliations.

Below is a high-level architecture diagram:

  • Service Cloud V2 → Integration Suite → S/4HANA

Key Features of the ESM-WorkZone Integration

  1. Centralized Access:

    • The ESM Self-Service Portal is embedded within SAP WorkZone under the Employee Self-Service (ESS) page.

  2. Knowledge Base Access:

    • Employees can optionally search Knowledge Base (KB) articles for relevant information before creating a case.

  3. Structured Attachments:

    • The system enforces consistent structure for attachments and forms, ensuring all required information is included with the case.

  4. Streamlined Case Creation:

    • Employees can create cases using a pre-configured JE form, making the process intuitive and efficient.

Employee Case Management Process

  • Case Creation: Once employees log into the WorkZone ESS page.The company accountant seamlessly initiates an accrual posting request through the Employee Self-Service widget on the SAP Build Work Zone portal, ensuring all essential details are accurately captured and submitted with ease.

 

 

  • Once an Accountant submits an accrual posting request, the Finance Support Agent receives an instant real-time alert about the new case assignment. This streamlined notification system ensures quick access to the case and enables efficient management, allowing finance teams to respond promptly and maintain smooth operational workflows.

  • The Finance Support Agent carefully examines the details of each newly created case, reviewing key aspects such as case type, employee information, and categorization. This thorough assessment ensures accurate classification, enabling swift and efficient resolution.

  • Leveraging advanced AI capabilities, Finance Support Agents can now access dynamically generated summaries that consolidate essential case details. These intelligent summaries provide a clear, concise overview of critical information, empowering agents to make faster, more informed decisions and deliver efficient resolutions.

  • The Finance Support Agent meticulously reviews the Case Form to ensure all details are accurate and complete. Once verified, they seamlessly process and post the data as a journal entry in SAP S/4HANA, maintaining precise and efficient financial records. This streamlined workflow not only upholds data integrity but also enhances overall operational efficiency in financial management.

  • Once the journal entry is posted, the Finance Support Agent accesses the SAP S/4HANA Journal Entry interface to review and validate the recorded details. This crucial step ensures the accuracy of the data and allows for the swift identification and resolution of any discrepancies.

 

  • When a Journal Entry is posted, the system seamlessly updates the case status to "Completed." This automation streamlines case management, ensuring efficient workflows and accurate case closures.

 

Benefits of ESM-WorkZone Integration

  1. Enhanced Employee Experience:

    • By centralizing access and enabling self-service capabilities, employees can address their concerns without unnecessary delays.

  2. Operational Efficiency:

    • Automation of accrual postings and structured case management reduces manual errors and administrative overhead.

  3. Knowledge Empowerment:

    • Access to the Knowledge Base empowers employees to find answers independently, reducing case volume.

  4. Comprehensive Case Visibility:

    • Integration with SAP WorkZone ensures that employees and agents can track case status in real time.

  5. Compliance and Accuracy:

    • Seamless posting to SAP S/4HANA ensures financial accuracy and compliance with organizational policies.

Real-World Application

Consider an employee needing reimbursement for an approved expense:

  1. The employee logs into WorkZone ESS, searches for relevant KB articles, and submits the JE form with supporting documents.
  2. The case is created in ESM, capturing all necessary details.
  3. HR agents review the case, validate the attachments, and post the accrual to SAP S/4HANA.
  4. The case status is updated in WorkZone, and the employee is notified of the resolution.

This streamlined process ensures transparency, efficiency, and employee satisfaction.

Conclusion

Integrating SAP ESM with WorkZone simplifies employee case management by providing a unified platform for case creation, tracking, and resolution. Whether it’s troubleshooting common issues, posting accruals, or closing cases, this integration ensures that both employees and HR teams have the tools they need for efficient service delivery.

Ready to transform your employee service operations? Start leveraging the power of SAP ESM and WorkZone today!

entry.title: Make The Most Out of Your SAP CX Solutions with CQC Services
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community! 

If you're working with SAP Customer Experience (CX) solutions, you’ve probably heard about Continuous Quality Check (CQC) services. But what are they really about, and how can they help you? Let’s break it down in plain language.

What Are CQC Services?

Think of CQC services as a health check for your SAP systems. They’re designed to make sure everything is running smoothly and to catch potential issues before they become big problems. It’s like giving your car a tune-up before a long road trip—better safe than sorry, right?

For SAP CX solutions, there are two key CQC services available:

  1. CQC for Business Process Performance Optimization (BPPO): This service is all about making things faster and smoother. It looks at the way your critical business processes run within SAP Sales and Service Cloud and offers advice on how to speed things up and avoid bottlenecks. If your team has ever felt frustrated with slow response times or workflow problems, this service is a game-changer. Note: This service is only available for SAP Sales and Service Cloud.

  2. CQC for Deployment Readiness: Whether you’re just about to go live with a new system or adding new features, this service ensures you’re set up for success. It checks your system’s configuration and analyzes things like security, performance, and stability. This service supports a wide range of SAP CX solutions—from SAP Sales and Service Cloud to SAP Commerce Cloud, SAP Customer Data Cloud, and SAP Emarsys. It’s like having an expert double-check everything before you flip the switch.

Why Should You Care?

Implementing or optimizing SAP systems can be a complex process, and nobody wants surprises during go-live or day-to-day operations. These CQC services give you peace of mind by catching potential issues early and providing actionable recommendations.

Here’s what you’ll get:

  • A detailed analysis of your system.

  • Practical tips to fix issues and improve performance.

  • Recommendations aligned with SAP’s best practices.

When to Use These Services

Not sure when to book a CQC? Here’s a quick guide:

  • CQC for BPPO: Use this when your system’s performance isn’t meeting expectations or when you’re handling complex processes that require fine-tuning.

  • CQC for Deployment Readiness: Book this before going live with a new implementation, adding new features, or as a health check for existing systems.

How to Get Started

Requesting a CQC service is simple. Just log in to the SAP for Me portal and select “Continuous Quality Check & Improvement Services” under the support options. Pro tip: SAP recommends booking the service about six weeks before your go-live date to ensure there’s enough time to make adjustments.

For more details, check out the information sheets for CQC for BPPO and CQC for Deployment Readiness to see how each service can help you.

Final Thoughts

At the end of the day, SAP’s CQC services for CX solutions are about making your life easier. Whether it’s optimizing performance or ensuring a smooth deployment, these services help you stay ahead of the game. So, why not give your SAP systems the care they deserve?


Easy steps, amazing CX!

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

About the author:

Edmar Rodríguez is a Topic Expert for the SAP Customer Experience Value Map. He helps customers make the most of their SAP Enterprise Support maintenance contract by guiding them through the available tools and services at their disposal. 

Have questions or want to keep the conversation going? We’d love to hear from you! Feel free to reach out to the SAP Customer Experience Value Map team at SAPESValueMaps@sap.com. And if you haven’t yet, sign up for the SAP Enterprise Support Value Maps to explore more resources, best practices, and expert guidance.

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

entry.title: SAP Sales and Service Cloud 2502 Release Briefing
entry.content:
Dear Customers,

We are happy to share the release briefing content of our SAP Sales and Service Cloud (aka Cloud for Customer) for the 2502 (February 2025) release. 

Click here for the release briefing content. 

Best regards

your Sales and Service Cloud Product Management

entry.title: Release Navigator for SAP Customer Experience - Q1 2025 Update
entry.content:
Welcome to 2025! As we embark on this new year filled with innovations, our team is thrilled to share the first update of the year for the Release Navigator for SAP Customer Experience. The content curated in this tool is intended to help you stay informed about the latest enhancements in your products from the Customer Experience portfolio. Quarterly updates will be provided to explore new features.

In this blog post, you can read about the update plans for the 2025 year and also which release content changes have been made in Q1 for product management teams.

Like 2024, we will continue to deliver content updates for the Release Navigator for SAP Customer Experience on a quarterly basis before product releases in February, May, August, and November. This regular schedule ensures that you will consistently receive the latest updates and content, which are important for your smooth run projects.

Each quarterly update is maintained for products that follow the harmonized release schedule: SAP Sales Cloud, SAP Marketing Cloud, SAP Service Cloud, and SAP Emarsys. You can find the specific product release dates featured on the Product-Specific page within the release navigator. Moreover, the only UX update for 2025 is planned for August. This update is focused on making your interaction with our platform even more intuitive and seamless, thereby streamlining your workflow and boosting overall efficiency.

Disclaimer: These dates are informational purposes only and may change. The Readiness@Scale team has the final decision over any UX modifications.

For SAP Sales Cloud and SAP Service Cloud, there will be a new update format. Instead of traditional release webcasts, a blog post will be published in advance, with detailed written documentation on current enhancements and improvements. This will allow you to access information at your own pace, ensuring flexibility and convenience. The release content for SAP Marketing Cloud will slightly change moving forward. The team will continue to host release webcasts, offering opportunities to engage with experts and learn about new features. However, they will not create a blog post for the upcoming releases. SAP Emarsys offers a release webcast series throughout the entire 2025 year, and you can find the link on the product page. Additionally, you will always find the URL for the current release webcast there for quicker access.

In addition to the specific content changes for 2502 in the release navigator, for products that follow a specific release schedule, resources are also available for products operating on a continuous delivery model. These include

  1. SAP Sales Version 2
  2. Service Cloud Version 2
  3. SAP Commerce Cloud.

Recap information for these products is provided after each quarter. More specific information is always available on the respective product pages.

As we continuously enhance the release navigator for you, we plan to add new content to the SAP Commerce Cloud product page in the near future. Stay tuned for updates.

With the help of badges introduced last year, you can easily identify new content, content that is about to be retired, or content of high importance on the release navigator.

Badges

The Readiness@Scale is a cross-solution team responsible for release readiness topics in the SAP Enterprise Support portfolio. Access all the release navigators at Release Ready page and find our team blogs with the tag Readiness@Scale.

If you have any feedback or specific requests regarding the release navigator, we would be more than happy to hear from you. We appreciate all feedback and value your input.

Readiness@Scale with SAP Enterprise Support
